title: Custom Visualization with ggplot2 and plotly
text: ’s simulation tibble looks like: row one body one point time. Every column available plotting, filtering, analysis. Since just tibble, full power dplyr ggplot2 disposal.

title: Custom Visualization with ggplot2 and plotly
text: example, Earth-Moon system, plot_orbits() shows overlapping circles bodies orbit shared barycenter roughly scale. useful visualization might plot body’s distance barycenter time:  plot Moon’s path relative Earth color gradient showing passage time:
code: library(ggplot2)  sim |>   dplyr::mutate(r = sqrt(x^2 + y^2)) |>   ggplot(aes(x = time / 86400, y = r, color = id)) +   geom_line(linewidth = 1) +   labs(     title = "Distance from Barycenter Over Time",     x = "Time (days)",     y = "Distance (m)",     color = "Body"   ) +   theme_minimal() sim |>   shift_reference_frame("Earth", keep_center = FALSE) |>   ggplot(aes(x = x, y = y, color = time / 86400)) +   geom_path(linewidth = 1.2) +   scale_color_viridis_c(name = "Day") +   coord_equal() +   labs(title = "Lunar Orbit (Earth-Centered)", x = "X (m)", y = "Y (m)") +   theme_minimal()

title: Custom Visualization with ggplot2 and plotly
text: Just plot_orbits() quick convenience 2D work, plot_orbits_3d() quick convenience 3D. intentionally simple — get useful plot one line can focus physics, formatting. need control, simulation tibble works just well plotly ggplot2. example, color trajectories speed rather body: point ggplot2: simulate() returns standard tibble, full access plotly’s API anything built-plotting functions don’t cover.
code: library(plotly) #>  #> Attaching package: 'plotly' #> The following object is masked from 'package:ggplot2': #>  #>     last_plot #> The following object is masked from 'package:stats': #>  #>     filter #> The following object is masked from 'package:graphics': #>  #>     layout  sim <- create_system() |>   add_body("Earth", mass = mass_earth) |>   add_body("Moon",  mass = mass_moon,            x = distance_earth_moon,            vy = speed_moon * cos(5 * pi / 180),            vz = speed_moon * sin(5 * pi / 180)) |>   simulate(time_step = 3600, duration = 86400 * 28)  sim <- sim |>   dplyr::mutate(speed = sqrt(vx^2 + vy^2 + vz^2))  plot_ly() |>   add_trace(     data = dplyr::filter(sim, id == "Moon"),     x = ~x, y = ~y, z = ~z,     type = 'scatter3d', mode = 'lines',     line = list(       width = 5,       color = ~speed,       colorscale = 'Viridis',       showscale = TRUE,       colorbar = list(title = "Speed (m/s)")     ),     name = "Moon"   ) |>   add_trace(     data = dplyr::filter(sim, id == "Earth"),     x = ~x, y = ~y, z = ~z,     type = 'scatter3d', mode = 'lines',     line = list(width = 3, color = 'gray'),     name = "Earth"   ) |>   layout(     title = "Lunar Orbit Around Earth",     showlegend = FALSE,     scene = list(       xaxis = list(title = 'X (m)'),       yaxis = list(title = 'Y (m)'),       zaxis = list(title = 'Z (m)'),       aspectmode = "data"     )   )

title: Examples
text: standard 28-day lunar orbit. One-hour time steps.
code: create_system() |>   add_body("Earth", mass = mass_earth) |>   add_body("Moon",  mass = mass_moon, x = distance_earth_moon, vy = speed_moon) |>   simulate(time_step = 3600, duration = 86400 * 28) |>   plot_orbits()

title: Examples
text: full year daily time steps.
code: create_system() |>   add_body("Sun",   mass = mass_sun) |>   add_body("Earth", mass = mass_earth, x = distance_earth_sun, vy = speed_earth) |>   simulate(time_step = 86400, duration = 86400 * 365) |>   plot_orbits()

title: Examples
text: orbitr uses N-body gravity, nested hierarchies require special setup. Piggyback Moon’s initial conditions onto Earth’s using simple vector addition. Note scale, Earth Moon orbits overlap — Earth-Moon distance (~384,000 km) tiny compared Earth-Sun distance (~150 million km). Use shift_reference_frame("Earth") (shown next example) zoom Earth-Moon subsystem:
code: create_system() |>   add_body("Sun",   mass = mass_sun) |>   add_body("Earth", mass = mass_earth, x = distance_earth_sun, vy = speed_earth) |>   add_body("Moon",  mass = mass_moon,            x = distance_earth_sun + distance_earth_moon,            vy = speed_earth + speed_moon) |>   simulate(time_step = 3600, duration = 86400 * 365) |>   plot_orbits()

title: Examples
text: three-body plot heliocentric (Sun center). see Moon’s path Earth’s perspective, pipe results shift_reference_frame():
code: create_system() |>   add_body("Sun",   mass = mass_sun) |>   add_body("Earth", mass = mass_earth, x = distance_earth_sun, vy = speed_earth) |>   add_body("Moon",  mass = mass_moon,            x = distance_earth_sun + distance_earth_moon,            vy = speed_earth + speed_moon) |>   simulate(time_step = 3600, duration = 86400 * 365) |>   shift_reference_frame("Earth") |>   plot_orbits()

what: The Kepler-16 System: A Real Circumbinary Planet
title: Examples
text: Kepler-16b first confirmed planet orbiting two stars — real-life Tatooine. system K-type star (0.68 solar masses) M-type star (0.20 solar masses) orbiting every ~41 days, Saturn-sized planet orbiting pair 0.7 AU.
code: G <- 6.6743e-11 AU <- distance_earth_sun  # Star masses m_A <- 0.68 * mass_sun m_B <- 0.20 * mass_sun m_planet <- 0.333 * mass_jupiter  # Binary star orbit (~0.22 AU separation) a_bin <- 0.22 * AU r_A <- a_bin * m_B / (m_A + m_B) r_B <- a_bin * m_A / (m_A + m_B) v_A <- sqrt(G * m_B^2 / ((m_A + m_B) * a_bin)) v_B <- sqrt(G * m_A^2 / ((m_A + m_B) * a_bin))  # Planet orbit (0.7048 AU from barycenter) r_planet <- 0.7048 * AU v_planet <- sqrt(G * (m_A + m_B) / r_planet)  create_system() |>   add_body("Star A", mass = m_A, x = r_A, vy = v_A) |>   add_body("Star B", mass = m_B, x = -r_B, vy = -v_B) |>   add_body("Kepler-16b", mass = m_planet, x = r_planet, vy = v_planet) |>   simulate(time_step = 3600, duration = 86400 * 228.8 * 3) |>   plot_orbits()

title: Built-In Physical Constants
code: mass_sun          # 1.989e30 kg mass_earth        # 5.972e24 kg mass_moon         # 7.342e22 kg mass_mars         # 6.417e23 kg mass_jupiter      # 1.898e27 kg mass_saturn       # 5.683e26 kg mass_venus        # 4.867e24 kg mass_mercury      # 3.301e23 kg

title: Built-In Physical Constants
code: distance_earth_sun    # 1.496e11 m  (~149.6 million km) distance_earth_moon   # 3.844e8  m  (~384,400 km) distance_mars_sun     # 2.279e11 m distance_jupiter_sun  # 7.785e11 m distance_venus_sun    # 1.082e11 m distance_mercury_sun  # 5.791e10 m

title: Built-In Physical Constants
code: speed_earth       # 29,780 m/s speed_moon        #  1,022 m/s speed_mars        # 24,070 m/s speed_jupiter     # 13,060 m/s speed_venus       # 35,020 m/s speed_mercury     # 47,360 m/s

title: Built-In Physical Constants
text: means Earth-Moon example can written :
code: create_system() |>   add_body("Earth", mass = mass_earth) |>   add_body("Moon",  mass = mass_moon, x = distance_earth_moon, vy = speed_moon) |>   simulate(time_step = 3600, duration = 86400 * 28) |>   plot_orbits()

title: Built-In Physical Constants
text: Orbital distances truly constant — every orbit ellipse, separation two bodies changes continuously throughout revolution. values provided semi-major axes: average closest approach (periapsis) farthest point (apoapsis). semi-major axis single characteristic length scale elliptical orbit. determines orbital period via Kepler’s Third Law, paired mean orbital speed, produces near-circular trajectory closely approximates real orbit. example, Earth-Sun distance varies 147.1 million km January (perihelion) 152.1 million km July (aphelion). semi-major axis 149.6 million km sits right middle gives correct one-year orbital period. want elliptical orbit instead, start body periapsis faster--mean velocity, apoapsis slower one.

title: 3D Plotting
text: plot_orbits() checks whether (sim_data$z != 0). ’s Z-axis movement — pass three_d = TRUE — hands plot_orbits_3d(), uses plotly interactive 3D rendering. Otherwise falls back static ggplot2 chart. plotly isn’t installed, function warns falls back 2D plot even 3D data.

title: 3D Plotting
text: Moon’s real orbit inclined 5° ecliptic. can approximate giving Moon small vz component: vz non-zero, plot_orbits() detects 3D motion returns interactive plotly widget. can drag rotate, scroll zoom, hover see timestamps.
code: create_system() |>   add_body("Earth", mass = mass_earth) |>   add_body("Moon",  mass = mass_moon,            x = distance_earth_moon,            vy = speed_moon * cos(5 * pi / 180),            vz = speed_moon * sin(5 * pi / 180)) |>   simulate(time_step = 3600, duration = 86400 * 28) |>   plot_orbits()

title: 3D Plotting
text: Even system entirely planar, can opt interactive 3D viewer: gives rotation zoom controls, even though Z values zero.
code: create_system() |>   add_body("Earth", mass = mass_earth) |>   add_body("Moon",  mass = mass_moon, x = distance_earth_moon, vy = speed_moon) |>   simulate(time_step = 3600, duration = 86400 * 28) |>   plot_orbits(three_d = TRUE)

title: Quick Start Guide
text: 3D interactive plotting, ’ll also want:
code: # install.packages("devtools") devtools::install_github("daverosenman/orbitr") install.packages("plotly")

title: Quick Start Guide
text: orbitr designed around simple pipe-friendly workflow: create system, add bodies, simulate, plot.  ’s — 28-day lunar orbit four lines. ’s step : create_system() initializes empty simulation standard gravitational constant G. add_body() places body given mass, position, velocity. positions meters, velocities m/s. built-constants (mass_earth, distance_earth_moon, etc.) save looking anything . simulate() runs N-body integration forward time. time_step many seconds per integration step, duration total time simulate. plot_orbits() produces quick 2D trajectory plot using ggplot2.
code: library(orbitr) #>  #> Attaching package: 'orbitr' #> The following object is masked from 'package:stats': #>  #>     simulate  create_system() |>   add_body("Earth", mass = mass_earth) |>   add_body("Moon",  mass = mass_moon, x = distance_earth_moon, vy = speed_moon) |>   simulate(time_step = 3600, duration = 86400 * 28) |>   plot_orbits()

title: Quick Start Guide
text: Since orbitr full N-body engine, can add many bodies want. one gravitationally interacts every . ’s Sun-Earth-Moon system full year:  Notice shift_reference_frame("Earth") — re-centers everything Earth can see Moon’s orbit instead everything overlap Sun’s scale.
code: create_system() |>   add_body("Sun",   mass = mass_sun) |>   add_body("Earth", mass = mass_earth, x = distance_earth_sun, vy = speed_earth) |>   add_body("Moon",  mass = mass_moon,            x = distance_earth_sun + distance_earth_moon,            vy = speed_earth + speed_moon) |>   simulate(time_step = 3600, duration = 86400 * 365) |>   shift_reference_frame("Earth") |>   plot_orbits()

title: Quick Start Guide
text: default integrator Velocity Verlet, conserves energy keeps orbits stable. can switch "euler_cromer" faster (less accurate) runs, "euler" see happens energy isn’t conserved:  ’ll see orbit spiral outward — ’s Euler method artificially pumping energy system. Switch back method = "verlet" clean closed ellipse.
code: create_system() |>   add_body("Star", mass = 1e30) |>   add_body("Planet", mass = 1e24, x = 1e11, vy = 30000) |>   simulate(time_step = 3600, duration = 86400 * 365, method = "euler") |>   plot_orbits()

what: The Output is Just a Tibble
title: Quick Start Guide
text: simulate() returns standard tidy tibble. can use dplyr, ggplot2, plotly, tool : row one body one point time, columns position (x, y, z), velocity (vx, vy, vz), mass, body ID, time.

title: Quick Start Guide
text: Physics — Understand math behind simulation Examples — complex systems including binary stars Kepler-16 system Unstable Orbits — random configurations chaotic 3D Plotting — Interactive 3D visualization plotly Custom Visualization — Build plots ggplot2 plotly Physical Constants — built-masses, distances, speeds

title: The Physics
text: Every body system attracts every body according Newton’s Law Universal Gravitation. body jj, net acceleration due bodies kk : →j=∑k≠jGmkrjk2r̂jk\vec{}_j = \sum_{k \neq j} \frac{G \, m_k}{r_{jk}^2} \, \hat{r}_{jk} rjk=|r→k−r→j|r_{jk} = \lvert\vec{r}_k - \vec{r}_j\rvert distance two bodies r̂jk\hat{r}_{jk} unit vector pointing jj toward kk.

title: The Physics
text: Gravity alone pull every body straight toward every body. prevents colliding initial velocity — sideways motion turns free-fall curved orbit. reason Moon doesn’t crash Earth: ’s falling toward us constantly, ’s also moving sideways fast enough keeps missing. call add_body(), vx, vy, vz parameters set initial velocity. balance speed distance determines shape orbit. given distance rr central mass MM, circular orbit velocity : vcirc=GMrv_{\text{circ}} = \sqrt{\frac{G \, M}{r}} body’s speed exactly matches , traces perfect circle. Faster orbit stretches ellipse (escapes entirely v≥vcirc2v \geq v_{\text{circ}} \sqrt{2}). Slower orbit drops tighter ellipse dips closer central body. zero velocity, body falls straight — orbit .

title: The Physics
text: two bodies pass close, r→0r \0 acceleration diverges toward infinity. well-known numerical problem N-body codes. orbitr offers optional softening length ε\varepsilon regularizes potential: rsoft=r2+ε2r_{\text{soft}} = \sqrt{r^2 + \varepsilon^2} softening enabled, close encounters produce large finite forces instead blowing NaN. Set softening = 0 (default) exact Newtonian gravity, try something like softening = 1e4 (10 km) dense systems.

title: The Physics
text: simulate() offers three methods stepping system forward time. operate 3D Cartesian coordinates.

title: The Physics
text: second-order symplectic integrator. conserves energy long timescales, making gold standard orbital mechanics. Orbits stay closed stable indefinitely. x→t+Δt=x→t+v→tΔt+12a→tΔt2\vec{x}_{t+\Delta t} = \vec{x}_t + \vec{v}_t \, \Delta t + \tfrac{1}{2} \vec{}_t \, \Delta t^2 v→t+Δt=v→t+12(→t+→t+Δt)Δt\vec{v}_{t+\Delta t} = \vec{v}_t + \tfrac{1}{2} \left( \vec{}_t + \vec{}_{t+\Delta t} \right) \Delta t position advanced first, acceleration recalculated new position, finally velocity updated using average old new accelerations. requires two acceleration evaluations per step (main cost), payoff stability enormous.

title: The Physics
text: first-order symplectic method. updates velocity first, uses new velocity update position. small reordering prevents systematic energy drift plagues standard Euler: v→t+Δt=v→t+→tΔt\vec{v}_{t+\Delta t} = \vec{v}_t + \vec{}_t \, \Delta t x→t+Δt=x→t+v→t+ΔtΔt\vec{x}_{t+\Delta t} = \vec{x}_t + \vec{v}_{t+\Delta t} \, \Delta t Faster Verlet (one acceleration evaluation per step) less accurate. Good quick previews.

title: The Physics
text: classical textbook method. Position velocity updated using values current time step: x→t+Δt=x→t+v→tΔt\vec{x}_{t+\Delta t} = \vec{x}_t + \vec{v}_t \, \Delta t v→t+Δt=v→t+→tΔt\vec{v}_{t+\Delta t} = \vec{v}_t + \vec{}_t \, \Delta t artificially pumps energy system, causing orbits spiral outward time. Included primarily educational comparison — use Verlet real work.

title: The Physics
text: Verlet traces clean closed ellipse, Euler-Cromer stays close drifts slightly, standard Euler spirals outward pumps energy orbit.
code: library(orbitr) #>  #> Attaching package: 'orbitr' #> The following object is masked from 'package:stats': #>  #>     simulate library(dplyr) #>  #> Attaching package: 'dplyr' #> The following objects are masked from 'package:stats': #>  #>     filter, lag #> The following objects are masked from 'package:base': #>  #>     intersect, setdiff, setequal, union  system <- create_system() |>   add_body("Star", mass = 1e30) |>   add_body("Planet", mass = 1e24, x = 1e11, vy = 30000)  verlet <- simulate(system, time_step = 3600, duration = 86400 * 365, method = "verlet") |>   mutate(method = "Velocity Verlet")  euler_cromer <- simulate(system, time_step = 3600, duration = 86400 * 365, method = "euler_cromer") |>   mutate(method = "Euler-Cromer")  euler <- simulate(system, time_step = 3600, duration = 86400 * 365, method = "euler") |>   mutate(method = "Standard Euler")  bind_rows(verlet, euler_cromer, euler) |>   filter(id == "Planet") |>   ggplot2::ggplot(ggplot2::aes(x = x, y = y, color = method)) +   ggplot2::geom_path(alpha = 0.7) +   ggplot2::coord_equal() +   ggplot2::theme_minimal()

title: The Physics
text: inner acceleration loop computational bottleneck N-body simulation. orbitr ships compiled C++ kernel (via Rcpp) computes O(n2)O(n^2) pairwise interactions tight nested loop. package installed source working C++ toolchain, simulate() automatically dispatches engine. compiled code isn’t available, falls back vectorized R implementation uses matrix outer products — still fast, C++ path significantly faster systems many bodies. can control use_cpp argument:
code: # Force the pure-R engine (useful for debugging or benchmarking) simulate(system, use_cpp = FALSE)

title: Unstable Orbits and the Three-Body Problem
text: ’s example: three equal-mass stars arranged triangle slightly asymmetric velocities. starts looking like interesting dance, asymmetry compounds eventually one stars get ejected:  actually happens real stellar dynamics — close three-body encounters star clusters frequently eject one star high velocity remaining two settle tighter binary. process called gravitational slingshot ejection.
code: create_system() |>   add_body("Star A", mass = 1e30, x = 1e11, y = 0, vx = 0, vy = 15000) |>   add_body("Star B", mass = 1e30, x = -5e10, y = 8.66e10, vx = -12990, vy = -7500) |>   add_body("Star C", mass = 1e30, x = -5e10, y = -8.66e10, vx = 14000, vy = -8000) |>   simulate(time_step = 3600, duration = 86400 * 365 * 10) |>   plot_orbits()

title: Unstable Orbits and the Three-Body Problem
text: simulations producing messy, diverging trajectories, things check assuming something wrong: Velocity high low. given distance rr central mass MM, circular orbit speed v=GM/rv = \sqrt{GM/r}. Deviating significantly produces eccentric orbits escape trajectories. Bodies close together. Close encounters produce extreme accelerations can blow numerically. Try increasing softening using smaller time_step. Three bodies. Chaos natural state N-body systems. stable examples documentation carefully tuned — don’t expect random configurations behave. Time step large. bodies move significant fraction orbital radius single step, integrator can’t track orbit accurately. Try halving time_step see result changes. built-constants examples orbitr designed give stable starting points. can tweak parameters watch system responds — ’s real intuition orbital mechanics comes .

what: A Tidy Physics Engine for Building and Visualizing Orbital Simulations
title: A Tidy Physics Engine for Building and Visualizing Orbital Simulations
text: tidy physics engine building visualizing orbital simulations. orbitr lightweight N-body gravitational physics engine built R ecosystem. Simulate planetary orbits, binary star systems, chaotic three-body problems lines pipe-friendly code. hood ships compiled C++ acceleration engine via Rcpp falls back gracefully fully vectorized pure-R implementation.
code: library(orbitr)  create_system() |>   add_body("Sun",   mass = mass_sun) |>   add_body("Earth", mass = mass_earth, x = distance_earth_sun, vy = speed_earth) |>   add_body("Moon",  mass = mass_moon,            x = distance_earth_sun + distance_earth_moon,            vy = speed_earth + speed_moon) |>   simulate(time_step = 3600, duration = 86400 * 365) |>   shift_reference_frame("Earth") |>   plot_orbits()

title: A Tidy Physics Engine for Building and Visualizing Orbital Simulations
text: 3D interactive plotting, ’ll also want:
code: # install.packages("devtools") devtools::install_github("DRosenman/orbitr") install.packages("plotly")

title: A Tidy Physics Engine for Building and Visualizing Orbital Simulations
text: workflow simple: create system, add bodies, simulate, plot. create_system() initializes empty simulation standard gravitational constant G. add_body() places body given mass, position, velocity. Built-constants like mass_earth distance_earth_moon save looking anything . simulate() runs N-body integration. default Velocity Verlet integrator conserves energy stable long-term orbits. plot_orbits() produces quick 2D trajectory plot — interactive 3D plotly visualization body Z-axis motion. output standard tidy tibble, can plug straight ggplot2, plotly, dplyr, whatever normally use.
code: create_system() |>   add_body("Earth", mass = mass_earth) |>   add_body("Moon",  mass = mass_moon, x = distance_earth_moon, vy = speed_moon) |>   simulate(time_step = 3600, duration = 86400 * 28) |>   plot_orbits()

title: A Tidy Physics Engine for Building and Visualizing Orbital Simulations

what: Add a physical body to the system — add_body
title: Add a physical body to the system — add_body
text: function introduces new celestial physical body `orbit_system`. must provide unique identifier mass. default, body placed origin (0, 0, 0) zero initial velocity unless specified.

title: Add a physical body to the system — add_body
code: add_body(system, id, mass, x = 0, y = 0, z = 0, vx = 0, vy = 0, vz = 0)

title: Add a physical body to the system — add_body
text: system `orbit_system` object created `create_system()`. id unique character string identify body (e.g., "Earth", "Apollo"). mass mass object kilograms. x Initial X-axis position meters (default 0). y Initial Y-axis position meters (default 0). z Initial Z-axis position meters (default 0). vx Initial velocity along X-axis meters per second (default 0). vy Initial velocity along Y-axis meters per second (default 0). vz Initial velocity along Z-axis meters per second (default 0).

title: Add a physical body to the system — add_body
text: updated `orbit_system` object containing newly added body.

title: Add a physical body to the system — add_body
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ my_universe <- create_system() |>   add_body(id = "Earth", mass = 5.97e24) |>   add_body(id = "Moon", mass = 7.34e22, x = 3.84e8, vy = 1022) } # }

what: Initialize an orbitr simulation system — create_system
title: Initialize an orbitr simulation system — create_system
text: Sets foundational data structure orbital simulation. Universal N-body gravity automatically integrated system using specified gravitational constant.

title: Initialize an orbitr simulation system — create_system
code: create_system(G = 6.6743e-11)

title: Initialize an orbitr simulation system — create_system
text: G gravitational constant. Defaults standard physics (6.6743e-11). simulate zero-gravity environment (inertia ), set `G = 0`.

title: Initialize an orbitr simulation system — create_system
text: empty `orbit_system` object ready bodies added.

title: Initialize an orbitr simulation system — create_system
code: # Creates a system with standard gravity my_universe <- create_system()  # Creates a universe with 10x stronger gravity heavy_universe <- create_system(G = 6.6743e-10)  # Creates a zero-gravity sandbox floating_universe <- create_system(G = 0)

title: Physical Constants for Orbital Mechanics — physical_constants
text: `mass_sun`: Mass Sun (1.989 x 10^30 kg). Source: IAU 2015 nominal solar mass. `mass_earth`: Mass Earth (5.972 x 10^24 kg). Source: IAU 2015 nominal Earth mass. `mass_moon`: Mass Moon (7.342 x 10^22 kg). Source: JPL DE440 ephemeris. `mass_mars`: Mass Mars (6.417 x 10^23 kg). Source: JPL DE440 ephemeris. `mass_jupiter`: Mass Jupiter (1.898 x 10^27 kg). Source: JPL DE440 ephemeris. `mass_saturn`: Mass Saturn (5.683 x 10^26 kg). Source: JPL DE440 ephemeris. `mass_venus`: Mass Venus (4.867 x 10^24 kg). Source: JPL DE440 ephemeris. `mass_mercury`: Mass Mercury (3.301 x 10^23 kg). Source: JPL DE440 ephemeris. `distance_earth_sun`: Semi-major axis Earth's orbit around Sun   (1.496 x 10^11 m, ~149.6 million km). Earth's actual distance varies   ~147.1 million km (perihelion) ~152.1 million km (aphelion). `distance_earth_moon`: Semi-major axis Moon's orbit around Earth   (3.844 x 10^8 m, ~384,400 km). Moon's actual distance varies   ~363,300 km (perigee) ~405,500 km (apogee). `distance_mars_sun`: Semi-major axis Mars's orbit around Sun   (2.279 x 10^11 m, ~227.9 million km). Mars notably eccentric orbit   (e = 0.093), ranging ~206.7 million km ~249.2 million km. `distance_jupiter_sun`: Semi-major axis Jupiter's orbit around Sun   (7.785 x 10^11 m, ~778.5 million km). `distance_venus_sun`: Semi-major axis Venus's orbit around Sun   (1.082 x 10^11 m, ~108.2 million km). Venus circular orbit   planet (e = 0.007). `distance_mercury_sun`: Semi-major axis Mercury's orbit around Sun   (5.791 x 10^10 m, ~57.9 million km). Mercury eccentric planetary   orbit (e = 0.206), ranging ~46.0 million km ~69.8 million km. `speed_earth`: Mean orbital speed Earth around Sun (29,780 m/s). `speed_moon`: Mean orbital speed Moon around Earth (1,022 m/s). `speed_mars`: Mean orbital speed Mars around Sun (24,070 m/s). `speed_jupiter`: Mean orbital speed Jupiter around Sun (13,060 m/s). `speed_venus`: Mean orbital speed Venus around Sun (35,020 m/s). `speed_mercury`: Mean orbital speed Mercury around Sun (47,360 m/s).

title: Physical Constants for Orbital Mechanics — physical_constants
text: Orbital distances truly constant. Every orbit ellipse, separation two bodies changes continuously. distances provided **semi-major axes** — average closest approach (periapsis) farthest point (apoapsis). semi-major axis single characteristic length scale elliptical orbit: determines orbital period via Kepler's Third Law, paired circular velocity distance, produces near-circular orbit closely approximates real trajectory. example, Earth-Sun distance varies 147.1 million km (perihelion January) 152.1 million km (aphelion July). semi-major axis 149.598 million km sits right middle gives correct orbital period one year.

title: Plot Orbital Trajectories (Smart 2D/3D Dispatch) — plot_orbits
text: Plot Orbital Trajectories (Smart 2D/3D Dispatch)

title: Plot Orbital Trajectories (Smart 2D/3D Dispatch) — plot_orbits
code: plot_orbits(sim_data, three_d = FALSE)

title: Plot Orbital Trajectories (Smart 2D/3D Dispatch) — plot_orbits
text: sim_data tibble output `simulate()` three_d Logical. TRUE, forces 3D plot even 2D data.

title: Plot 3D Interactive Orbital Trajectories — plot_orbits_3d
text: Generates interactive 3D visualization orbital system using plotly. can click, drag rotate, scroll zoom trajectories.

title: Plot 3D Interactive Orbital Trajectories — plot_orbits_3d
code: plot_orbits_3d(sim_data)

title: Plot 3D Interactive Orbital Trajectories — plot_orbits_3d
text: sim_data tibble containing simulation output `simulate()`.

title: Plot 3D Interactive Orbital Trajectories — plot_orbits_3d
text: plotly HTML widget displaying 3D orbits.

title: Plot 3D Interactive Orbital Trajectories — plot_orbits_3d
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ create_system() |>   add_body("Earth", mass = 5.972e24) |>   add_body("Moon", mass = 7.342e22, x = 3.844e8, vy = 1022, vz = 150) |>   simulate(time_step = 3600, duration = 86400 * 30) |>   plot_orbits_3d() } # }

what: Shift the coordinate reference frame of the simulation — shift_reference_frame
title: Shift the coordinate reference frame of the simulation — shift_reference_frame
text: Recalculates positions velocities bodies relative specific target body. effectively "anchors camera" chosen body, placing origin (0, 0, 0) time steps.

title: Shift the coordinate reference frame of the simulation — shift_reference_frame
code: shift_reference_frame(sim_data, center_id, keep_center = TRUE)

title: Shift the coordinate reference frame of the simulation — shift_reference_frame
text: sim_data tidy `tibble` containing output `simulate()`. center_id character string ID body use new origin. keep_center Logical. central body remain dataset (0 coordinates) removed? Default `TRUE`.

title: Shift the coordinate reference frame of the simulation — shift_reference_frame
text: tidy `tibble` updated `x`, `y`, `z`, `vx`, `vy`, `vz` columns.

title: Shift the coordinate reference frame of the simulation — shift_reference_frame
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Simulate Sun-Earth-Moon orbit_data <- create_system() |>   add_body("Sun", mass = 1.989e30) |>   add_body("Earth", mass = 5.97e24, x = 1.496e11, vy = 29780) |>   add_body("Moon", mass = 7.34e22, x = 1.496e11 + 3.84e8, vy = 29780 + 1022) |>   simulate(time_step = 3600, duration = 86400 * 365)  # Shift view to Earth and plot orbit_data |>   shift_reference_frame(center_id = "Earth") |>   plot_orbits() } # }

what: Simulate kinematics for an orbitr system — simulate
title: Simulate kinematics for an orbitr system — simulate
text: Propagates physical state `orbit_system` time using numerical integration. engine supports multiple mathematical methods, defaulting energy-conserving Velocity Verlet algorithm ensure highly stable orbital trajectories.

title: Simulate kinematics for an orbitr system — simulate
code: simulate(   system,   time_step = 60,   duration = 86400,   method = "verlet",   softening = 0,   use_cpp = TRUE )

title: Simulate kinematics for an orbitr system — simulate
text: system `orbit_system` object created `create_system()`. time_step time increment per frame seconds (default 60s). duration Total simulation time seconds (default 86400s / 1 day). method numerical integration method: "verlet" (default), "euler_cromer", "euler". softening small distance (meters) added prevent numerical singularities bodies pass close . gravitational distance computed `sqrt(r^2 + softening^2)` instead `r`. Default 0 (softening). value like 1e4 (10 km) reasonable planetary simulations. use_cpp Logical. `TRUE` (default), uses compiled C++ acceleration engine better performance. Falls back vectorized R C++ code available.

title: Simulate kinematics for an orbitr system — simulate
text: tidy `tibble` containing physical state (time, id, mass, x, y, z, vx, vy, vz)   every body every time step.

title: Simulate kinematics for an orbitr system — simulate
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ my_universe <- create_system() |>   add_body("Earth", mass = 5.97e24) |>   add_body("Moon", mass = 7.34e22, x = 3.84e8, vy = 1022) |>   simulate(time_step = 3600, duration = 86400 * 28) } # }
